questions.question: Explain the Von Neumann architecture and its limitations.
keyPoints: Single memory for both instructions and data
Sequential instruction processing
Von Neumann bottleneck due to shared bus for instructions and data
Limits on throughput when CPU and memory speeds differ

questions.question: Describe the functions of the three main types of system buses.
keyPoints: Data bus carries actual data between components
Address bus carries memory addresses for read/write operations
Control bus carries timing and control signals like read/write enable, interrupt requests

questions.question: Explain how interrupts improve CPU utilization in I/O operations.
keyPoints: CPU does not continuously poll devices
CPU continues processing other tasks while waiting for I/O
Device sends interrupt signal when ready
CPU services interrupt only when needed, improving efficiency

questions.question: What is the role of cache memory in computer systems?
keyPoints: Acts as intermediate storage between CPU and main memory
Stores frequently accessed data and instructions
Reduces memory access latency
Utilizes temporal and spatial locality principles
Multiple levels (L1, L2, L3) with varying speeds and sizes

questions.question: Differentiate between programmed I/O and interrupt-driven I/O.
keyPoints: Programmed I/O: CPU continuously checks device status (polling)
Interrupt-driven I/O: Device notifies CPU when ready
Programmed I/O wastes CPU cycles waiting
Interrupt-driven I/O allows CPU to do useful work during I/O wait

questions.question: Explain the bootstrap process in computer system organization.
keyPoints: Power-on self test (POST) initializes hardware
Bootstrap loader in ROM is executed
Hardware initialization and configuration
Operating system kernel is loaded into memory
Control transfers to OS, starting multi-tasking environment

questions.question: Compare and contrast symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) and asymmetric multiprocessing systems.
keyPoints: SMP: All processors equal, share memory and I/O equally
Asymmetric: Processors have specific roles, master-slave relationship
SMP provides better load balancing and scalability
Asymmetric is simpler to implement but less flexible
SMP requires sophisticated OS for coordination
Both improve performance but have different complexity trade-offs

questions.question: Explain DMA (Direct Memory Access) and its advantages over programmed I/O for data transfers.
keyPoints: DMA allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory
DMA controller manages transfer without CPU intervention
CPU can perform other computations during transfer
Reduces CPU overhead significantly
Useful for high-speed devices like disks and network cards
Involves setup, data transfer, and completion phases
Only one bus cycle per data item needed instead of multiple CPU cycles

questions.question: Describe the Harvard architecture and explain why it overcomes the Von Neumann bottleneck.
keyPoints: Harvard has separate instruction and data memories
Can fetch instruction and data simultaneously
Eliminates contention for the same memory bus
True parallelism in instruction execution
Modified Harvard variants allow some flexibility
Suitable for real-time and signal processing applications
Trade-off: more complex hardware and higher cost

questions.question: Explain the organization of memory hierarchy and why it is essential for modern computer performance.
keyPoints: Registers (fastest, smallest) → Cache → Main Memory → Secondary Storage (slowest, largest)
Each level balances speed, cost, and capacity
Temporal locality: recently accessed items likely accessed again
Spatial locality: items near recently accessed items likely accessed soon
Hit rate determines effective access time
Cache mapping techniques: direct, associative, set-associative
Essential because fast memory is expensive and vice versa
